description:
You are a capacity-planner who has been asked to update two small configuration files and to create a concise report showing the final values that will be used by the monitoring team.

FILES AND LOCATIONS (all paths are under /home/user)

1.  /home/user/projects/capplan/resources.yaml
    • Format: standard YAML.  
    • Current keys: “servers” (a YAML sequence of server maps).  
    • What to add: a NEW server map with the following exact key/value pairs (order does not matter in YAML but the key spellings do):
        name: cache01
        cpu: 1
        memory: 4

2.  /home/user/projects/capplan/thresholds.toml
    • Format: valid TOML with two tables: [cpu] and [memory].  
    • What to change:
        a) In table [cpu] set warning = 65  (was 70)  
        b) In table [memory] set critical = 90 (was 95)

3.  /home/user/projects/capplan/planner.log   ← YOU must create this file
    • Plain UTF-8 text, newline-delimited.  
    • Exact layout (six lines, in this order, no extra spaces at line ends):
        SERVER db01 CPU 4 MEM 16
        SERVER web01 CPU 2 MEM 8
        SERVER cache01 CPU 1 MEM 4
        THRESHOLD cpu WARNING 65 CRITICAL 90
        THRESHOLD memory WARNING 75 CRITICAL 90

      – Each token is separated by a single space.  
      – No header line, no trailing blank line.

YOUR GOALS

A. Insert the “cache01” server into resources.yaml.  
B. Edit thresholds.toml to apply the new warning / critical numbers exactly as listed above.  
C. Produce planner.log with the exact six lines shown.  
D. Use no more than about four shell commands in total (the testing harness checks command count, so combine steps where reasonable).

The automated checker will fail if:
• resources.yaml is not valid YAML or is missing the new server block.  
• thresholds.toml is not valid TOML or does not contain the new numeric values.  
• planner.log is missing, has extra/less lines, wrong ordering, or stray whitespace.

Hints (do NOT output these commands verbatim):
• Standard Unix text-manipulation tools (echo, printf, tee, sed) are usually enough.  
• Remember to preserve the rest of each file exactly as found—only the requested parts may change.  
• Run a YAML/TOML linter if unsure.

When everything is finished, you may cat planner.log so the result is visible in the console, but this is optional.
truth:
Initial filesystem before agent starts:

Directory: /home/user/projects/capplan   (already exists, writable by user)

1. /home/user/projects/capplan/resources.yaml
------------------------------------------------
servers:
  - name: db01
    cpu: 4
    memory: 16
  - name: web01
    cpu: 2
    memory: 8
------------------------------------------------

2. /home/user/projects/capplan/thresholds.toml
------------------------------------------------
[cpu]
warning = 70
critical = 90

[memory]
warning = 75
critical = 95
------------------------------------------------

Desired final filesystem state after agent succeeds:

1. /home/user/projects/capplan/resources.yaml
------------------------------------------------
servers:
  - name: db01
    cpu: 4
    memory: 16
  - name: web01
    cpu: 2
    memory: 8
  - name: cache01
    cpu: 1
    memory: 4
------------------------------------------------

2. /home/user/projects/capplan/thresholds.toml
------------------------------------------------
[cpu]
warning = 65
critical = 90

[memory]
warning = 75
critical = 90
------------------------------------------------

3. /home/user/projects/capplan/planner.log
------------------------------------------------
SERVER db01 CPU 4 MEM 16
SERVER web01 CPU 2 MEM 8
SERVER cache01 CPU 1 MEM 4
THRESHOLD cpu WARNING 65 CRITICAL 90
THRESHOLD memory WARNING 75 CRITICAL 90
------------------------------------------------

The automated test will:
1. Read resources.yaml as YAML and confirm three server entries (db01, web01, cache01) with the exact cpu/memory ints shown above.
2. Read thresholds.toml via a TOML parser and confirm the exact numeric values:
   cpu.warning == 65
   cpu.critical == 90
   memory.warning == 75
   memory.critical == 90
3. Check planner.log byte-for-byte against the five-line block above (including newline at end of file being optional).

No other files may be altered inside /home/user/projects/capplan.
